title: Car Washes

narrative.executive_view: Car washes have durable paid demand and an active consolidation market, but they are a weak pure-AI value-migration thesis. The Census scope is establishments primarily cleaning, washing or waxing vehicles, including detail, mobile and self-service washes [S1]. At the attractive express end, the real assets are the site, tunnel and membership book: Mister reported that subscriptions produced 76% of 2025 wash sales [S2]. AI can improve membership recognition, marketing and overhead, but it does not perform the wash and should not receive credit for decades-old tunnel mechanization. A buyer should underwrite this as a location, subscription and real-estate investment with a modest AI overlay.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The clearest AI-enabled tasks are license-plate recognition, frictionless member access, payment, customer-service triage, marketing segmentation, scheduling and multi-site reporting. Current AI-LPR products automate member signup and transaction processing and analyze usage patterns [S5]. Those tools touch attendants, cashiers, customer-service staff, office clerks and managers, but most of the wage bill remains physical cleaning, loading, maintenance and site supervision. The supplied occupational mix is a 5-digit proxy that includes oil-change businesses, so automotive technicians and tire workers are largely sibling-industry contamination and are assigned little AI replaceability rather than treated as car-wash labor.
narrative.value_capture: Distribution is favorable because customers pay the operator directly, without a payer or administered fee schedule. Retention is less certain: location, visible wash quality and convenience matter, and Mister warns that competition can force price cuts or service changes [S2]. Membership improves recurrence, yet industry data show 7.2% average monthly churn and stronger retention only at larger member bases [S3]. The result is high operator control over realized savings but only a middling moat: nearby tunnel supply, promotions and churn can pass part of any efficiency gain back to consumers.
narrative.adoption_timing: Digital and automated workflows are already visible in scaled express operators, but effective AI adoption is well below 50% across the full NAICS population. AI-LPR is commercially available and is being included in new systems [S5], while the largest public chain already operates a technology-intensive subscription model [S2]. The long independent tail, heterogeneous formats and limited payoff on physical roles slow diffusion. A four-year estimate to 50% effective adoption reflects relatively easy software deployment but not wholesale labor substitution.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The market is active but more selective than at the 2022 peak. Three directly evidenced acquirers are Mister, Whistle Express and Quick Quack [S7][S8][S9], while the specialist M&A report describes broader buyer participation and a turn toward densification and bolt-ons [S4]. For 5–20-site, real-estate-owned, high-volume portfolios, Car Wash Advisory reported 2025 values around 8–10× EBITDA [S4]. The selected 8.5× entry and 9.0× exit sit inside that same observed range; the exit premium assumes a better diversified, professionally managed portfolio, not a return to peak 10–12× pricing. EBITDA must be normalized for market rent, maintenance capital and membership churn.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable: mature AI does not clean vehicles, and recurring road use continues to soil them. The larger risk is economic, not technological elimination. New local supply can impair volume and pricing, customers can cancel subscriptions at any time [S2], and weather causes seasonal variation [S3]. Self-service, mobile detailing and express tunnels all remain within the exact NAICS scope [S1], but their labor intensity and capital requirements differ sharply, so durable category demand does not make every format or site durable.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are format mix, the 5-digit occupational proxy, real-estate treatment and the transferability of scaled-chain evidence to lower-middle-market targets. A leased tunnel at market rent is not economically comparable with a real-estate-owned portfolio, and the cited 8–10× evidence is specifically for 5–20-site, high-volume, real-estate-owned deals [S4]. Owner-age evidence is a cross-industry employer-business proxy, not car-wash-specific [S6]. AI adoption evidence is product and operator evidence rather than a representative penetration survey. These limitations warrant medium overall confidence and wide ranges around adoption, owner age and multiples.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition — 811192 Car Washes
sources.what_it_supports: Defines the exact industry as establishments primarily cleaning, washing and/or waxing vehicles, including detail shops, mobile washes and self-service washes.

sources.title: Mister Car Wash 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Reports 548 locations, a primarily express-exterior model, 76% of wash sales from subscriptions, acquisition activity, labor importance and competitive pricing and churn risks.

sources.title: Rinsed Releases New Quarterly Car Wash Industry Report
sources.what_it_supports: Reports same-location member and retail revenue trends, member lifetime by scale and 7.2% average monthly churn.

sources.title: ICA 2025 Presentation: M&A & Capital Markets
sources.what_it_supports: Reports consolidation trends and 2025 valuation of roughly 8–10× EBITDA for 5–20-site, real-estate-owned, high-volume car-wash portfolios, versus 10–12× at the 2022 peak.

sources.title: Are You Keeping Up with the Digitization of the Car Wash?
sources.what_it_supports: Describes AI-enabled license-plate recognition, automated recognition and gate activation, integrated payment, targeted marketing and the continuing role of pay stations; trade-association partner content.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.what_it_supports: Reports Census-based evidence that 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and documents broad succession-planning gaps.

sources.title: Mister Car Wash Acquires Five Stores from Whistle Express
sources.what_it_supports: Documents Mister Car Wash as an active 2025 acquirer.

sources.title: Whistle Express Signs Definitive Agreement to Acquire Take 5 Car Wash
sources.what_it_supports: Documents Whistle Express as an active acquirer and the announced combination's 530-location scale.

sources.title: Quick Quack Car Wash Expands Footprint in Texas with Acquisition of Washboys Express
sources.what_it_supports: Documents Quick Quack as an active 2025 acquirer.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI reaches customer access, payments, CRM, scheduling and analysis, but not the physical wash, detailing or equipment maintenance. Fractions are bounded task-share judgments within the supplied roles, with sibling-code technical roles kept low.
caveats: The role mix is 5-digit NAICS 81119 and includes oil-change/lube operations; automotive technicians and tire workers are sibling-code contamination.
Tunnel mechanization is mature capital equipment and is deliberately not credited as AI replacement.

role_judgments.rationale: Cleaning and detailing are physical; only incidental documentation and workflow prompts automate.
Kiosks and LPR reduce payment and membership tasks, but loading, guiding and prep remain on site.
Repair is physical and this role is largely lube-shop contamination; diagnostics and paperwork are the limited AI surface.
Scheduling, labor planning and reporting can compress supervisory time, while safety and site execution persist.
Multi-site analytics, pricing, marketing and reporting automate materially, but leadership and local accountability remain.
Kiosk, app and LPR payment can remove much transactional work, with service recovery and upselling retained.
Planning and documentation assist, but supervision of physical maintenance remains.
Physical tire work is outside core car washing and minimally AI-replaceable.
Membership questions, billing triage and routine communications are well suited to automation, with escalations retained.
Routine records, reconciliation and correspondence can be heavily automated.
The unlisted tail mixes marketing and administration with drivers and physical support, so only a minority is replaceable.

pi_dist.rationale: The operator controls retail and subscription pricing directly; there is no payer or regulated fee schedule between the wash and consumer.
caveats: Direct pricing control does not prevent competitive discounting; that separate retention risk is reflected in pi_moat.

pi_moat.rationale: Convenient locations and memberships support repeat use, but cancellation is easy and local entrants can compete on price and service.
caveats: Mister is a scaled express proxy and may overstate membership resilience for independent or legacy-format targets.

t50_years.rationale: AI-enabled access and workflow tools can deploy quickly, but the fragmented long tail and small addressable task pool slow effective adoption across the industry.
caveats: No representative NAICS-811192 AI-adoption survey was found; the estimate bridges product availability and scaled-operator practice to the full industry.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Scaled express chains use data-intensive memberships and AI-LPR is commercially available, but usage is not shown to cover a majority of operators or replaceable tasks.
caveats: Technology availability and chain deployment are proxies for effective labor-task adoption, not measured penetration.

historical_analogs.value: Kiosk and app payment, subscription CRM and license-plate recognition; tunnel mechanization is excluded as non-AI capital automation.
historical_analogs.rationale: The closest demonstrated analogs digitize transactions and customer identification without removing the physical production process.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: From limited front-counter displacement to broad cashier/CRM automation, with little effect on wash labor
caveats: The analog mixes traditional automation with current machine-learning features and is not evidence that AI automates the tunnel itself.

selection.rationale: A downward adjustment from the 52.3% share of employer businesses owned by people 55 or older approximates the narrower 60-plus threshold.
caveats: Cross-industry U.S. employer-business proxy; it is not specific to car washes and reports age 55+, not 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Broad U.S. succession gaps are documented, but no car-wash-specific shortage evidence was verified.
caveats: Absence of verified industry-specific evidence is not proof that individual sellers lack succession pressure.

active_consolidators.rationale: A conservative floor counts only three separately verified strategic buyers with 2025 acquisition announcements: Mister, Whistle Express and Quick Quack.
caveats: This is a verified lower bound, not a census of all private-equity platforms or regional acquirers.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected near the lower-middle of the reported 2025 range of roughly 8–10× EBITDA for 5–20-site, real-estate-owned, 100,000-plus-cars-per-year portfolios.
caveats: The source cohort is high-volume, real-estate-owned and larger than many targets; leased, single-site or legacy formats can price materially lower.
The multiple must be applied to normalized EBITDA after market rent and recurring maintenance capital.

exit_mult.rationale: A scaled, diversified portfolio with strong membership metrics is assigned the midpoint of the same observed 8–10× current-market range, only 0.5× above entry and below the 2022 peak range.
caveats: The modest exit premium depends on creating a portfolio-grade asset; no return to peak 10–12× pricing is assumed.
Exit value is highly sensitive to real-estate ownership, leverage, local saturation, membership churn and capital-market conditions.

pricing_structure.value: Direct-to-consumer single-wash and cancelable monthly membership pricing, with strong local competition and no third-party payer.
pricing_structure.rationale: Public-operator and industry data show a membership-led retail model whose economics depend on recurring fees, churn and local demand.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: From cash-heavy single-wash/self-service sites to express operators with most wash sales from subscriptions
caveats: Mister's 76% subscription share is a scaled-chain benchmark and not representative of every NAICS 811192 format.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The modeled AI surface is modest and concentrated in transactional and administrative roles; a four-year adoption horizon does not convert mature tunnel automation into AI value.

terminal_value.justification: Vehicles still require physical cleaning; AI changes access, marketing and administration rather than eliminating paid wash demand.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Exact NAICS scope is anchored to Census [S1]. Role weights remain a disclosed 5-digit OEWS proxy with oil-change contamination; owner age and adoption are explicitly disclosed proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Local tunnel oversupply, easy membership cancellation and promotions can pass savings to consumers; this is reflected in pi_moat rather than terminal demand.

dataset_mismatch.description: The occupational mix is 5-digit NAICS 81119, not exact 811192, and includes automotive repair/lube roles; labor share also combines 2024 wages and 2022 receipts.
sensitivity: Removing contaminated technician and tire roles changes little because their AI fractions are near zero.
Express-only targets may have more cashier/attendant automation but lower total labor intensity than the all-format dataset.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Express-exterior multi-site operators for the $1–10M EBITDA acquisition thesis
market_boundary.selection_basis: The cited transaction multiples and subscription benchmarks are strongest for high-volume express portfolios; other exact-NAICS formats remain in scope but have materially different labor and valuation economics.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The adoption factor ranges from about 1.4 to 5.0, materially changing the composite score.
Format mix and the contaminated occupation proxy can move available labor value by roughly a factor of two.
An exit at or below entry eliminates the multiple-spread factor; a premium exit requires portfolio quality and favorable capital markets.

reviewer_note: Audit the 8–10× multiple cohort against the target's real-estate and site-volume profile, and do not treat tunnel mechanization as AI.
